titleStmt.title: 光化寺舊存佛名三則
Three Buddha Names, Formerly on the Boulder near Guanghua Monastery

availability.p: Demonstration set, published in the Epiwen public corpus; rights remain with the source providers.

sourceDesc.bibl: from the combined record (+ the three per-text records CLS_9.1–9.3). The upstream combined record is, functionally, the object record of the lost stone.

objectIdentifier.objectName: 光化寺舊存佛名三則刻石
Lost Buddha-names stone, formerly near Guanghua Monastery

msContents.summary: Three Buddha names (11 characters in three columns) of the 570 CE programme, on one stone now lost: 觀世音佛 · 阿彌陀佛 · 弥勒佛. Known only through the rubbings listed below.

msItem.title: 光化寺“觀世音佛”刻銘
“Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds” on the Boulder at Guanghua Monastery
光化寺“阿彌陀佛”刻銘
“Buddha Amitābha” on the Boulder at Guanghua Monastery
光化寺“弥勒佛”刻銘
“Buddha Maitreya” on the Boulder at Guanghua Monastery

material.term: 黑雲母– 角閃石 – 花崗岩

support.note: material stated on the per-text records CLS_9.1–9.3 (zh spacing verbatim); necessarily derived from pre-loss documentation.
the recorded 105 × 94 equals the sheet size of rubbing A (中國國家圖書館: 高105釐米，寬94釐米) — for a lost stone the measurement is OF THE WITNESS, not of the rock (witness-primacy).

layout.p: Three vertical name-columns on one field; combined glyph heights 14–55 cm (measured from the rubbing; the eleven glyphs are dimensioned on the upstream combined record, not on the per-text records).

provenance.p: 佚失（upstream condition, verbatim）；標題稱「舊存」。

object.note: A：徂徠山9號拓本。高105釐米，寬94釐米。中國國家圖書館藏品。刊於《北京圖書館藏中國歷代石刻拓本匯編》第8册：第17頁。 B：徂徠山9號拓本。高104釐米，寬106釐米。日本西川寧舊藏。刊於伏見冲敬 第7頁；西川寧和神田喜一郎第55－56頁。 C：徂徠山9號拓本。刊於関野貞和常盤大定第1册：圖108；重印於関野貞和常盤大定圖册11：第76頁，第3圖。 D：徂徠山9號拓本。臺北國家圖書館藏品。高110釐米，寬108釐米。

A: National Library of China, Beijing; 105 × 94 cm; published in 匯編 8: 17 · B: ex collection Nishikawa Yasushi 西川寧, Japan; 104 × 106 cm · C: published by Sekino Tadashi and Tokiwa Daijō (1: fig. 108; Plates 11: 76(3)) · D: National Central Library, Taibei; 110 × 108 cm. These witnesses are the ONLY access to the lost stone; the per-text records CLS_9.1–9.3 point back to them.
